reasoning: The borders in Kwara state (specifically Taberu to Tanu) were closed by Benin on May 7, 2025, due to alleged agricultural protectionism, arms smuggling, and the emergence of a new terror group (‘Mahmuda’). Rather than resolving the issue, Benin escalated the situation on May 21, 2025, by expanding the border clampdown to a waterway in Ogun State and key borders in Lagos (Sèmè, Owode), citing a 'national security strategy.' Given the severe stated reasons (terrorism, arms smuggling) and the broader geopolitical context of this expansion, it is highly unlikely that diplomatic efforts will succeed in completely reversing the policy and reopening the Kwara borders within the short remaining timeframe (30 days until July 1, 2025). Historically, West African border closures involving national security or major diplomatic rifts take many months to resolve. While the Nigerian House of Representatives has called for diplomatic intervention, there is no evidence of a breakthrough as of May 31, 2025.
